home.title: TradeAutomation Research Dashboards
home.intro: Versioned static dashboards for LP + Force Strike experiments. Use stable version pages for comparison instead of overwriting old analysis.

current_baseline.title: Current Baseline: LP3 take-all all timeframes
current_baseline.description: V13 relaxed the old hard guardrails and selected take-all as the current research baseline: LP3 across H4/H8/H12/D1/W1, 0.5 signal-candle pullback, FS structure stop, single 1R target, and fixed 6-bar pullback wait. This has higher return and shorter underwater than cap 4R, but requires accepting same-symbol stacking and higher concurrent exposure.

pages.title: V1 Baseline: Broad Permutation Test
pages.question: Which first-pass entry and exit families are worth studying after combining LP breaks with raw Force Strike signals?
pages.setup: Broad permutation test across M30, H4, D1, and W1 using next-open and midpoint-pullback entries, structure stops, ATR width filters, and fixed R targets.
pages.how_to_read: Treat this as exploration. M30 dominates the signal count, so compare timeframes before reading the aggregate leaderboard.
pages.conclusion: Midpoint-pullback entries were materially better than next-open entries. M30 was weak, while H4, D1, and W1 justified a focused follow-up.
pages.action: Use only as historical context. V2 and later pages contain the refined research path.

pages.title: V2 Focus: H4/D1/W1 Midpoint Entry
pages.question: Does the midpoint-pullback family still work when M30 is removed and ATR risk-width filters are compared?
pages.setup: Focused H4, D1, and W1 test using midpoint-pullback entries, Force Strike structure stops, ATR max-risk variants, and fixed R targets.
pages.how_to_read: Use the focused timeframe tables and ignore M30 because it is not part of this run.
pages.conclusion: The midpoint-pullback structure-stop family stayed positive across H4, D1, and W1. The 1ATR and 1.25ATR variants were close.
pages.action: Use V2 to understand why V3 moved into entry-zone and partial-exit testing.

pages.title: V3 Entry/Exit: Zones, ATRs, Partial Exits
pages.question: Which signal-candle entry zone, ATR risk filter, and exit structure is strongest before stability testing?
pages.setup: H4, D1, and W1 test of 0.5, 0.6, and 0.7 signal-candle pullbacks, ATR max-risk filters, single-target exits, and MT5-portable partial exits.
pages.how_to_read: Compare model families first, then check per-timeframe robustness. This run has many simulated candidate trades because each signal is tested against many permutations.
pages.conclusion: The 0.5 signal-candle pullback was strongest. Single-target 1R beat the tested partial-exit variants for the best individual candidate.
pages.action: Use the 0.5 zone, structure stop, 1R family as the base candidate for later tests.

pages.title: V4 Stability: Train/Test Symbol Filters
pages.question: Can train-period symbol/timeframe filters improve the later test period for the best V3 candidates?
pages.setup: Walk-forward stability test using V3 trades, a 2023 split, and several training-period filters for symbol/timeframe inclusion.
pages.how_to_read: Read the test period first. A filter is useful only if it improves later unseen performance, not just training results.
pages.conclusion: The learned symbol/timeframe filters did not improve the test period. The unfiltered baseline remained better.
pages.action: Do not add symbol/timeframe filters yet. Keep the weak pockets visible but avoid this filtering method.

pages.title: V5 H8 Bridge: Native MT5 H8 Test
pages.question: Does native MT5 H8 provide a useful bridge between H4 and D1?
pages.setup: Single candidate test using 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, 1R target, and native MT5 H8 data.
pages.how_to_read: Compare H8 against H4 and D1. The aggregate is less important than the quality step between timeframes.
pages.conclusion: H8 improved modestly over H4 but did not become a clean midpoint between H4 and D1.
pages.action: Keep H8 available for comparison, but do not pivot the main strategy research around H8.

pages.title: V6 H12 Bridge: Native MT5 H12 Test
pages.question: Does native MT5 H12 provide a better bridge between H8 and D1?
pages.setup: Single candidate test using 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, 1R target, and native MT5 H12 data.
pages.how_to_read: Use this as the current timeframe comparison snapshot. Compare H4, H8, H12, D1, and W1 independently.
pages.conclusion: H12 is a meaningful bridge. It is stronger than H8 and closer to D1 quality while keeping more trade count than D1.
pages.action: Keep H12 in the forward research set. Use V6 as the current timeframe snapshot, not V7 or V8.

decision_brief.headline: V6 result: H12 is worth keeping in the research set.
key_results: D1/W1 remain cleaner, but H12 adds meaningful frequency with materially better quality than H8.
decision_brief.next_step: Keep H12 in later portfolio studies instead of treating H4/H8/D1/W1 as the only practical set.

pages.title: V7 Entry Wait: Cancel At Would-Be 1R
pages.question: Should a pullback order stay open until entry or be cancelled once the would-be 1R target is reached first?
pages.setup: Same 0.5 pullback, structure stop, 1R target model, but pending entry waits until either the pullback fills or the would-be 1R target is reached. If both happen in one candle, V7 cancels first.
pages.how_to_read: This is not a new baseline. It is a rule challenge against the fixed 6-bar pullback wait.
pages.conclusion: Do not replace the fixed 6-bar pullback wait. V7 is unusable because the cancel-first same-bar rule removes too many valid trades.
pages.action: Do not use V7 for strategy selection. Keep the fixed 6-bar entry wait.

decision_brief.headline: V7 result: reject the cancel-first 1R entry-wait rule.
key_results: Fixed 6-bar baseline: 13,012 trades, 58.0% win rate, 0.116R avg, PF 1.265, 1,512.3R.
V7 cancel-first: 4,885 trades, 35.7% win rate, -0.321R avg, PF 0.519.
The cancel-first same-bar rule removes too many valid pullback entries.
This is not a drawdown improvement tradeoff; it is a materially worse model.
decision_brief.next_step: Do not use V7; keep the fixed 6-bar pullback wait.

pages.title: V8 Entry Wait: Entry-Priority 1R Cancel
pages.question: If a candle touches both pullback entry and would-be 1R, does entry-first handling make the 1R-cancel wait rule usable?
pages.setup: Same test as V7, but if entry and would-be 1R occur in the same candle, the setup fills first instead of cancelling first.
pages.how_to_read: V8 is the fairer version of the 1R-cancel wait rule, but it must beat the fixed 6-bar baseline to replace it.
pages.conclusion: Do not replace the fixed 6-bar pullback wait. V8 is positive but weaker than the baseline on trade count, win rate, average R, PF, and total R.
pages.action: Do not use V8 as the active baseline. Keep the fixed 6-bar entry wait and use V6 for timeframe comparison.

decision_brief.headline: V8 result: positive, but still weaker than the baseline.
key_results: Fixed 6-bar baseline: 13,012 trades, 58.0% win rate, 0.116R avg, PF 1.265, 1,512.3R.
V8 entry-first: 9,627 trades, 55.3% win rate, 0.062R avg, PF 1.133, 598.3R.
Entry-first handling fixes the worst V7 flaw, but the 1R-cancel wait still cuts too much profitable opportunity.
The fixed 6-bar wait remains better on trade count, win rate, average R, PF, and total R.
decision_brief.next_step: Do not replace the fixed 6-bar pullback wait.

pages.title: V9 LP Pivot Strength Sensitivity
pages.question: Which LP pivot strength performs best when the current fixed 6-bar baseline trade model is kept constant?
pages.setup: All 28 FX major/cross pairs across H4, H8, H12, D1, and W1. Only LP pivot strength changes: 2, 3, 4, and 5. Lookbacks remain H4 30D, H8 60D, H12 180D, D1 1Y, and W1 4Y.
pages.how_to_read: Read the LP Pivot Strength table and the candidate heatmap by timeframe. The aggregate is useful only after confirming that one pivot strength is not carried by a single timeframe.
pages.conclusion: LP pivot 5 has the best overall Avg R and PF, while LP pivot 4 is the more balanced quality/count compromise. LP pivot 2 produces the most trades but weakest quality, and LP pivot 3 is no longer the best quality default for this trade model.
pages.action: Do not treat LP 5 as final execution default yet. Use LP 5 for quality-focused follow-up and LP 4 as the balanced comparison candidate.

decision_brief.headline: V9 result: higher LP pivots improve quality, but reduce trade count.
key_results: LP5 has the best quality: 0.134R avg and PF 1.310.
LP4 is the balanced quality/count candidate: 9,512 trades, 0.129R avg, PF 1.297.
LP3 remains a strong baseline: 13,012 trades, 0.116R avg, PF 1.265.
LP2 has the most total R, but weakest quality, so it should not become the practical default.
decision_brief.next_step: Use portfolio guardrails before changing the LP default.

pages.title: V10 Portfolio Baseline: Take-All vs Exposure Caps
pages.question: Does a practical portfolio exposure cap improve drawdown and underwater behavior enough to replace taking every LP + Force Strike trade?
pages.setup: Existing V9 trades only. LP3, LP4, and LP5 are tested across all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1 trades. V10 compares take-all against 4R, 6R, 8R, and 10R max-open-risk caps with one open trade per symbol.
pages.how_to_read: Start with Best Practical Mechanics. A row must pass 30R max drawdown and 180D longest underwater before total R is allowed to choose the winner.
pages.conclusion: Only LP3 with the 4R exposure cap passed both V10 guardrails. Take-all had higher Total R, but breached the 30R max drawdown guardrail. LP4 and LP5 kept attractive quality metrics, but their underwater periods were too long under the 180D rule.
pages.action: Use LP3 cap 4R as the current practical portfolio baseline. Move next to V11 timeframe mix testing using this exposure rule.

decision_brief.headline: V10 result: LP3 cap 4R is the practical portfolio baseline.
key_results: LP3 take-all produced 1,512.3R, but breached the 30R drawdown guardrail with 33.4R max DD.
LP3 cap 4R produced 1,100.9R, 26.7R max DD, and 162D underwater, passing both guardrails.
LP4 and LP5 kept attractive PF, but their underwater periods were too long under the 180D rule.
The 4R cap sacrifices some total R, but it makes the model portfolio-feasible under the current guardrails.
decision_brief.next_step: Use LP3 cap 4R for timeframe-mix testing.

pages.title: V11 Practical Timeframe Mix
pages.question: Should the V10 LP3 cap 4R portfolio keep trading all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1 signals, or should weaker lower-timeframe exposure be removed?
pages.setup: Existing V9 trades only. V10 mechanics stay fixed: LP3, 4R max-open-risk cap, one open trade per symbol, higher-timeframe same-time priority, and 30R / 180D guardrails. V11 tests practical timeframe baskets and limited LP4/LP5 diagnostics.
pages.how_to_read: Start with Best Timeframe Mix. Then compare the H4/H8 Decision table and Underwater Reduction table before using total R alone.
pages.conclusion: Keep all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1 timeframes for the current LP3 cap 4R baseline. The all-timeframe row still passed guardrails and produced the highest Total R. Removing H4 or H8 lowered drawdown, but the return sacrifice or underwater miss was not worth replacing the baseline.
pages.action: Keep all timeframes for the current practical baseline. Use V12 to retest LP3/LP4/LP5 on all timeframes and the cleaner no-H4 diagnostic set.

decision_brief.headline: V11 result: keep the current baseline as LP3 cap 4R across all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1.
key_results: All TF LP3 cap 4R: 10,037 trades, 1,100.9R, max DD 26.7R, underwater 162D, pass.
Remove H4: lower DD/underwater, but gives up about 308R, not worth replacing baseline.
Remove H8: gives up about 157R and misses the underwater guardrail at 182.5D.
LP4/LP5 become viable only in no-H4 diagnostics, but with lower total R than LP3 all timeframes.
decision_brief.next_step: Keep all timeframes and finalize the LP pivot default.

pages.title: V12 LP Pivot Finalization
pages.question: Should the practical strategy keep LP3 as the default pivot strength, or should LP4/LP5 replace it after portfolio and timeframe mechanics are fixed?
pages.setup: Existing V9 trades only. V11 mechanics stay fixed: 4R max-open-risk cap, one open trade per symbol, all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1 timeframes, and 30R / 180D guardrails. V12 compares LP3, LP4, and LP5, with no-H4 as a robustness contrast.
pages.how_to_read: Start with All-Timeframe Pivot Decision. Then use Quality vs Profitability and No-H4 Robustness Contrast to decide whether higher PF is worth fewer trades and lower total R.
pages.conclusion: Keep LP3 as the practical default. LP4 and LP5 have attractive quality, but on the selected all-timeframe baseline they fail guardrails. The no-H4 contrast makes LP3/LP4/LP5 all pass, but LP3 still has the highest Total R.
pages.action: Use LP3 cap 4R across all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1 as the current research baseline. Future work should test execution constraints or risk sizing, not change the LP pivot default yet.

decision_brief.headline: V12 result: keep LP3 as the practical LP pivot default.
key_results: All-timeframe LP3: 10,037 trades, 1,100.9R, PF 1.248, max DD 26.7R, underwater 162D, pass.
All-timeframe LP4: 7,793 trades, 1,004.3R, PF 1.297, but fails with 34.4R max DD and 271D underwater.
All-timeframe LP5: 6,431 trades, 888.4R, PF 1.322, but fails underwater at 229D.
No-H4 makes LP3/LP4/LP5 all pass, but LP3 still has the highest total R.
decision_brief.next_step: Stop changing LP pivots for now; move to risk sizing, FTMO constraints, or execution-readiness.

pages.title: V13 Relaxed Portfolio Rule Selection
pages.question: Is cap 4R too conservative once the old 30R / 180D guardrails are treated as context instead of hard selection rules?
pages.setup: Existing V9 trades only. The signal and trade model stay fixed: LP3, all H4/H8/H12/D1/W1, 0.5 signal-candle pullback, full Force Strike structure stop, single 1R target, and fixed 6-bar pullback wait. V13 compares take-all, one-symbol no-cap, and cap 4R through cap 16R.
pages.how_to_read: Start with the Decision Brief, then read the Exposure Reality Check before treating take-all as tradable. V13 ranks by long-run Total R after checking yearly, ticker, and concentration robustness.
pages.conclusion: Use take-all as the current research baseline if account risk per trade is kept small enough. It produced the highest Total R, no negative years, no negative symbols, no obvious concentration skew, and shorter underwater than cap 4R. The cost is higher concurrent exposure and same-symbol stacking.
pages.action: Use take-all for the next research slice, but make execution constraints explicit: max 17 concurrent trades, max 4 same-symbol stack, and account DD examples of about 8.3% at 0.25% risk or 16.7% at 0.50% risk.

decision_brief.headline: V13 result: use take-all as the current research baseline, with explicit exposure caveats.
key_results: Take-all: 13,012 trades, 1,512.3R, max DD 33.4R, underwater 111D, 0 negative years, 0 negative symbols.
Cap 4R: 10,037 trades, 1,100.9R, max DD 26.7R, underwater 162D; it gives up about 411R and has a longer underwater period.
Cap 6R: 11,226 trades, 1,235.6R, max DD 32.6R, underwater 125D; it lowers exposure but still gives up about 277R versus take-all.
Take-all reached 17 concurrent trades and a max same-symbol stack of 4, so the next test must model account-level risk constraints before live execution.
decision_brief.next_step: Use take-all for the next strategy research slice, then test FTMO-style daily/max loss and practical execution constraints.
